ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies convolutional sentential representations to event coreference and avoids reliance on external knowledge bases, which is a useful engineering contribution but not a fundamentally new paradigm. Using two CNNs, concatenation with lexical/pairwise features, and a similarity layer feeding a logistic classifier is a fairly straightforward composition of existing components rather than a novel modeling framework. The creation of an extended ACE++ dataset adds value but does not by itself establish methodological novelty; the contribution is therefore best characterized as an incremental but well-differentiated advance over prior work. | B(3): This paper presents a practical, empirically useful application of convolutional sentence representations to event coreference, but the core idea—using neural sentential embeddings plus lexical/pairwise features and a similarity/classification layer—is an incremental adaptation of known techniques rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The explicit avoidance of external KBs and the creation of an extended ACE++ dataset are useful engineering contributions, and the paper clearly differentiates itself from prior work, but the methodological novelty beyond assembling CNN encoders with standard classifiers is moderate. An expert would find the application and dataset augmentation informative, but not surprising.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies convolutional sentential representations to event coreference and avoids reliance on external knowledge bases, which is a useful engineering contribution but not a fundamentally new paradigm. Using two CNNs, concatenation with lexical/pairwise features, and a similarity layer feeding a logistic classifier is a fairly straightforward composition of existing components rather than a novel modeling framework. The creation of an extended ACE++ dataset adds value but does not by itself establish methodological novelty; the contribution is therefore best characterized as an incremental but well-differentiated advance over prior work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper presents a practical, empirically useful application of convolutional sentence representations to event coreference, but the core idea—using neural sentential embeddings plus lexical/pairwise features and a similarity/classification layer—is an incremental adaptation of known techniques rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The explicit avoidance of external KBs and the creation of an extended ACE++ dataset are useful engineering contributions, and the paper clearly differentiates itself from prior work, but the methodological novelty beyond assembling CNN encoders with standard classifiers is moderate. An expert would find the application and dataset augmentation informative, but not surprising.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper reports hyperparameters and model configuration details (CNNs, similarity layer, optimizer) which helps reproduction, but the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described. One dataset (ACE 2005) is public but the authors’ ACE++ extension is ambiguously available, and code is not mentioned, making exact replication of training/preprocessing unlikely. Critically, the evaluation metrics and procedures are not specified in the summary, so replicators cannot be certain they are computing the same scores or evaluation splits. | B(3): The paper provides concrete model architecture and reports hyperparameters, which helps reimplementation, and uses ACE 2005 (public) plus an augmented ACE++ (one dataset reportedly public). However, the summary lacks a clear description of the data preprocessing pipeline, evaluation metrics/procedures are not specified, and code is not mentioned, making exact replication difficult. Given these gaps, reproduction is feasible but would require nontrivial guessing and engineering choices.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and model configuration details (CNNs, similarity layer, optimizer) which helps reproduction, but the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described. One dataset (ACE 2005) is public but the authors’ ACE++ extension is ambiguously available, and code is not mentioned, making exact replication of training/preprocessing unlikely. Critically, the evaluation metrics and procedures are not specified in the summary, so replicators cannot be certain they are computing the same scores or evaluation splits.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides concrete model architecture and reports hyperparameters, which helps reimplementation, and uses ACE 2005 (public) plus an augmented ACE++ (one dataset reportedly public). However, the summary lacks a clear description of the data preprocessing pipeline, evaluation metrics/procedures are not specified, and code is not mentioned, making exact replication difficult. Given these gaps, reproduction is feasible but would require nontrivial guessing and engineering choices.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized with clear early statement of contributions and a coherent flow from method (two CNNs, similarity layer, classifier) to experiments and conclusions; writing quality is reported as good and hyperparameters are documented, which aids reproducibility. However, the summary omits which evaluation metrics were used and does not mention whether figures/tables effectively illustrate results or if code/data (ACE++ release) are available, leaving some presentation gaps. Notation and definitions appear adequate given the methodological details provided (CNNs, features, loss), but the absence of explicit metric reporting is a notable clarity shortcoming. These are minor issues preventing a top score but overall presentation is strong. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: contributions (CNN-based sentential features avoiding KBs) are stated early and the structure follows intro → method → experiments → conclusion. Notation and methodological description appear consistent and readable, hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage and error analysis are adequate for reproducibility. Figures/tables/examples effectiveness is not fully detailed in the summary, so I infer minor presentation gaps (e.g., evaluation metric specification missing in the summary) but writing quality is described as good, so only small clarity improvements are needed.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized with clear early statement of contributions and a coherent flow from method (two CNNs, similarity layer, classifier) to experiments and conclusions; writing quality is reported as good and hyperparameters are documented, which aids reproducibility. However, the summary omits which evaluation metrics were used and does not mention whether figures/tables effectively illustrate results or if code/data (ACE++ release) are available, leaving some presentation gaps. Notation and definitions appear adequate given the methodological details provided (CNNs, features, loss), but the absence of explicit metric reporting is a notable clarity shortcoming. These are minor issues preventing a top score but overall presentation is strong.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: contributions (CNN-based sentential features avoiding KBs) are stated early and the structure follows intro → method → experiments → conclusion. Notation and methodological description appear consistent and readable, hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage and error analysis are adequate for reproducibility. Figures/tables/examples effectiveness is not fully detailed in the summary, so I infer minor presentation gaps (e.g., evaluation metric specification missing in the summary) but writing quality is described as good, so only small clarity improvements are needed.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of prior event-coreference systems (Chen & Ji 2009; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012; Sachan et al. 2015; Araki & Mitamura 2015), so relevant baselines are included. However, the summary does not state evaluation metrics or whether all experiments used identical conditions (and one primary dataset is an author-extended ACE++), raising fairness and reproducibility concerns; code is not mentioned. The manuscript reports hyperparameters and provides related-work discussion and an error analysis, but it is unclear whether it explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods match or outperform theirs or presents statistical testing across runs. Overall the comparisons are strong but missing some clarity on fairness/reproducibility and significance testing. | B(4): The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (Chen & Ji 2009; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012; Sachan et al. 2015; Araki & Mitamura 2015), which covers key prior work in event coreference. However, the summary does not state the evaluation metrics or confirm that all methods were evaluated under identical conditions, so I cannot verify full fairness of the comparisons. The paper includes error analysis and discusses related work, but the writeup does not explicitly report whether or when prior methods perform comparably, nor whether any more recent neural baselines were included — hence a small deduction for minor omissions in comparators and clarity on experimental parity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of prior event-coreference systems (Chen & Ji 2009; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012; Sachan et al. 2015; Araki & Mitamura 2015), so relevant baselines are included. However, the summary does not state evaluation metrics or whether all experiments used identical conditions (and one primary dataset is an author-extended ACE++), raising fairness and reproducibility concerns; code is not mentioned. The manuscript reports hyperparameters and provides related-work discussion and an error analysis, but it is unclear whether it explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods match or outperform theirs or presents statistical testing across runs. Overall the comparisons are strong but missing some clarity on fairness/reproducibility and significance testing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable set of established baselines (Chen & Ji 2009; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012; Sachan et al. 2015; Araki & Mitamura 2015), which covers key prior work in event coreference. However, the summary does not state the evaluation metrics or confirm that all methods were evaluated under identical conditions, so I cannot verify full fairness of the comparisons. The paper includes error analysis and discusses related work, but the writeup does not explicitly report whether or when prior methods perform comparably, nor whether any more recent neural baselines were included — hence a small deduction for minor omissions in comparators and clarity on experimental parity.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and delivers a practical, KB-independent approach that achieves state-of-the-art results and is likely to be adopted by practitioners. Reviewer concerns about limited diagnostic analyses, statistical testing, and potential reproducibility limits temper claims of transformational impact, but the demonstrated gains, dataset extension, and reported hyperparameters make the contribution likely to influence follow-up work, so a slightly above-median impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (event coreference) and reports state-of-the-art gains using CNN-derived sentential features without external KBs, which makes it a useful contribution. However, from the summary there is no evidence of the kinds of probing/interpretability analyses or comprehensive ablation studies I require to understand what the learned sentential representations capture, nor are statistical significance tests or clear reproducibility guarantees (code/dataset release) reported; one dataset is the authors’ extension which may limit external validation. Given these strengths and methodological omissions, the likely impact is moderate: useful and likely to be built upon, but not transformative without stronger diagnostic and reproducibility work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important and practical problem of event coreference and demonstrates a strong, KB-independent approach using sentential CNN features that achieves state-of-the-art results on ACE 2005 and the authors' extended ACE++ dataset. From a pragmatic research perspective, showing that learned sentential representations plus lightweight lexical/pairwise features can replace reliance on external knowledge bases is likely to change engineering choices and be adopted in follow-up work. The creation of an extended ACE++ corpus (with at least one public release) and the provided hyperparameter details increase the work's reproducibility and chance of reuse. While not paradigmatic, the paper meaningfully advances methods and opens useful directions for practitioners and researchers.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers strong empirical results, hyperparameter details, and an error analysis, but lacks key experimental transparency such as code release, clear evaluation metrics, statistical significance/multiple-run variance, and ablation/probing of major model components. Because these omissions limit reproducibility and make it hard to attribute gains to specific design choices, the work is solid but not sufficiently substantiated to merit a higher score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports strong empirical results on ACE2005 and an extended ACE++ set and provides hyperparameter details and an error analysis, which supports its core claim to some extent. However, the summary indicates no explicit ablation or probing analyses of the two CNNs, similarity layer, or lexical/pairwise feature contributions, and it does not report statistical significance or multiple-run variance for the main comparisons — deficiencies I penalize as a theory-driven methodologist. Related work and limitations are reasonably covered, but reproducibility is weak (no mention of released code and one dataset is authors’ extended data), so the experimental depth is adequate but missing key analyses and reproducibility guarantees.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical results (claims state-of-the-art on ACE 2005 and the authors' ACE++ extension), compares against several reasonable baselines, and includes hyperparameter reporting plus an error analysis — all of which align with a practical, reproducible focus. However, key experimental details in the summary are missing (evaluation metrics not specified, code availability not mentioned) and it’s unclear how extensive ablation/sensitivity analyses are beyond hyperparameter reporting. Given the public release of at least one dataset variant and adequate related-work coverage, the study is strong overall but has minor gaps in transparency and experimental detail that prevent a top score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper states clear claims and demonstrates empirical gains with a plausible, well-implemented method, and there are no apparent fundamental technical errors. However, they also consistently note missing ablation studies, statistical significance testing, interpretability analyses, and unclear reproducibility (ACE++ and code release), which weaken validation though do not undermine the core contributions. Given the solid empirical results but practical evaluation and reporting gaps, a slightly above-midscore of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear claims and experimental gains using CNN-derived sentential features and reports state-of-the-art results on ACE2005 and an extended ACE++ set, which supports its main empirical claim. However, from the summary I see no evidence of required ablation studies isolating each major component (two CNNs, similarity layer, lexical/pairwise features), no probing/interpretability analyses to justify what the sentential representations capture, and no mention of statistical significance testing across multiple runs. Reproducibility is also unclear (ACE++ creation and code availability not specified). These methodological gaps mean the results are promising but not yet fully validated, hence a moderate technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art performance on ACE 2005 and the authors' extended ACE++ dataset, and the methodology (dual CNN sentential features + lexical/pairwise features with a learned similarity and classifier) is technically plausible and implemented with reported hyperparameters. However, the summary omits crucial evaluation details (evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing) and there is no mention of released code, which weakens reproducibility and makes it harder to fully verify empirical claims. There are no apparent mathematical errors (no formal derivations were required), and the experimental comparisons include reasonable baselines, so the issues are practical rather than foundational.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a core NLP problem with an appropriate neural approach and datasets, making it relevant to top NLP/ML venues. I scored it a 4 because, while the empirical contribution and inclusion of an extended dataset are valuable, the paper lacks some important evaluation and reproducibility details (explicit metrics, significance testing, systematic ablations/probing, and code release) that should be clarified or added to meet the highest standards.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (event coreference with neural sentential features) is squarely within top NLP/ML venues and the paper targets the right audience, comparing to appropriate baselines and reporting hyperparameters. However, from the summary key methodological/evaluation expectations for these venues appear missing: evaluation metrics are not specified, there is no mention of systematic ablation or probing of the learned sentential representations, no statistical significance testing across runs, and code release is not stated. Given these reproducibility and analysis gaps the paper is borderline appropriate without stronger empirical rigor and interpretability analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (event coreference) using neural methods and reports state-of-the-art results on ACE 2005 and an expanded ACE++—a clear match for a top NLP/ML venue. The methodology (CNN-based sentential representations combined with lexical/pairwise features) and empirical focus align with the community’s expectations, and the inclusion of an extended dataset and error analysis is valuable for practitioners. My only reservations are practical: ACE 2005 is small (which the authors acknowledge) and code/reproducibility is not mentioned, and evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary—minor issues that warrant clarification but do not outweigh the paper’s relevance and empirical contribution.

main_claims: Proposes an event coreference (event linking) model that does not rely on external knowledge bases but uses sentential features produced by convolutional neural networks.

Uses two CNNs to generate latent sentential representations for event mentions, augments these with lexical and pairwise features, and trains a similarity function and classifier to produce coreference scores.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on two datasets (ACE 2005 and the authors' extended ACE++ dataset, one of which is publicly available).
Provides an error analysis highlighting directions for future research.
_summary.methodology: Two convolutional neural networks produce latent sentential feature representations for event-mention candidates; these are concatenated with lexical and pairwise features, passed through a similarity neural layer and a logistic regression classifier to generate coreference scores, trained with logistic loss using Adam.
limitations: The ACE 2005 corpus used is small (599 documents), which motivated creation of the ACE++ dataset.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-driven Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record of publishing formal models of discourse and structured representation learning.
traits: Penalises papers that lack formal or theoretical justification for model choices (e.g., no analysis of what the learned sentential representations capture) — will deduct points if no probing/interpretability analysis is provided.
Requires comprehensive ablation studies: subtracts score if any major component (each CNN, similarity layer, lexical/pairwise features) is not individually ablated and reported.
Demands statistical significance testing over multiple runs for main comparisons — will downgrade empirical claims if results are reported from single runs without confidence intervals or tests.
Penalises partial or private evaluation data: will lower score if the primary improvements rely on a dataset or preprocessing that is not fully released or reproducible.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on principled justification and diagnostic evaluations; generous to conceptual novelty but strict about theoretical analysis, ablations, and rigorous statistical validation.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Empiricalist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP and information extraction, experienced in building and evaluating end-to-end systems for entity/event coreference in real-world text.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks: gives positive credit for state-of-the-art results on ACE 2005 and an augmented ACE++ if evaluation appears sound.
Favors engineering approaches that reduce dependence on external resources (e.g., knowledge bases): will raise score for models achieving strong performance without KBs.
Values practical evaluation and error analysis: awards points if the paper includes realistic datasets, detailed error analysis, and ablation ranges that show when the method helps in practice.
Prefers reproducibility and code/data release: will boost score if at least one dataset and implementation details or code are publicly available (but is not as harsh if some parts are private).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Practical and results-oriented: emphasizes empirical gains, usefulness of the approach in real settings, and reproducibility; more forgiving of missing formal analysis if experiments are thorough and convincing.
